Isolate a.a: The variable to be isolated or solved for in the algebraic expression.
Isolate a.b: A variable or constant in the algebraic expression.
Isolate a.c: A variable or constant in the algebraic expression.

Expand the binomial.__power_1: This is the binomial expression $(x + 1)^2$ that is being expanded.
Expand the binomial.x: This is a variable within the binomial expression.

Factor the difference of squares.__power_2: This is the square of the variable $a$.
Factor the difference of squares.a: This is a variable being squared.
Factor the difference of squares.__power_4: This is the square of the variable $b$.
Factor the difference of squares.b: This is a variable being squared.

Relativistic energy–momentum.__power_2: This represents the square of the total relativistic energy $E$ of a particle.
Relativistic energy–momentum.E: This represents the total relativistic energy of a particle.
Relativistic energy–momentum.__power_4: This represents the square of the rest energy of a particle, $(mc^2)^2$.
Relativistic energy–momentum.m: This represents the invariant mass (or rest mass) of the particle.
Relativistic energy–momentum.__power_6: This represents the square of the speed of light in a vacuum.
Relativistic energy–momentum.c: This represents the speed of light in a vacuum.
Relativistic energy–momentum.__power_7: This represents the square of the product of the speed of light and the relativistic momentum, $(cp)^2$.
Relativistic energy–momentum.p: This represents the relativistic momentum of the particle.
Relativistic energy–momentum._r1___power_2: This represents the total relativistic energy $E$, derived from the particle's momentum and rest mass.
Relativistic energy–momentum.__power_5: This represents the square of the invariant mass of the particle.
Relativistic energy–momentum._r1___power_6: This represents the speed of light in a vacuum raised to the fourth power, $c^4$.
Relativistic energy–momentum.__power_10: This represents the inverse of the square of the product of the speed of light and the invariant mass, $\frac{1}{c^2 m^2}$.

Lorentz time dilation.__power_2: The square of the distance traveled by light in the stationary frame over time $t$.
Lorentz time dilation.c: The constant speed of light in a vacuum.
Lorentz time dilation.t: The time interval measured by an observer in the stationary frame.
Lorentz time dilation.__power_5: The square of the distance traveled by light in the moving frame over proper time $t_0$.
Lorentz time dilation.t_0: The proper time interval measured by an observer in the moving frame, at rest relative to the event.
Lorentz time dilation.__power_7: The square of the distance traveled by the moving object in the stationary frame over time $t$.
Lorentz time dilation.v: The constant relative velocity between the two inertial frames.
Lorentz time dilation.__power_3: The square of the speed of light, $c$.
Lorentz time dilation.__power_4: The square of the time interval $t$ measured in the stationary frame.
Lorentz time dilation._r1___power_7: The square of the speed of light, $c$.
Lorentz time dilation.__power_8: The square of the proper time interval $t_0$.
Lorentz time dilation.__power_10: The square of the relative velocity $v$.
Lorentz time dilation.__power_11: The square of the time interval $t$ measured in the stationary frame.
Lorentz time dilation._r1___power_11: The reciprocal of the square of the speed of light, $c^2$.

Quadratic roots & discriminant.a: The coefficient of the $x^2$ term in the standard form of a quadratic equation, $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.__power_5: The squared variable term in the quadratic equation.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.x: The variable in the quadratic equation.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.b: The coefficient of the $x$ term in the standard form of a quadratic equation, $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.c: The constant term in the standard form of a quadratic equation, $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.__power_7: The reciprocal of the coefficient $a$, used to normalize the quadratic equation during derivation.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.__power_9: The reciprocal of the coefficient $a$, used to normalize the quadratic equation during derivation.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.__power_8: A term derived from completing the square, representing the square of half the coefficient of $x$ after dividing by $a$.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.__power_10: A factor that emerges in the denominator of the quadratic formula.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.__power_17: A term derived from completing the square, representing the square of half the coefficient of $x$ after dividing by $a$.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.__power_19: A factor that emerges in the denominator of the quadratic formula.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.__power_13: The square of the coefficient $b$, appearing as part of the discriminant.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.__power_14: A term resulting from squaring $1/(2a)$, used in the process of completing the square.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.d0___power_8: The square root of the discriminant, which determines the nature of the roots of the quadratic equation.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.d1___power_4: A factor that appears in the denominator of the quadratic formula.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.d1___power_9: The square root of the discriminant, which determines the nature of the roots of the quadratic equation.
Quadratic roots & discriminant.d1___power_14: The square of the coefficient $b$, appearing as part of the discriminant.

Allen–Eggers entry velocity.v: The instantaneous velocity of the object undergoing atmospheric entry.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.t: Time elapsed during the atmospheric entry process.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.__deriv_2: The time rate of change of the object's velocity, representing its acceleration.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.rho: The atmospheric density at a given altitude.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.__power_5: The square of the object's velocity, often related to drag forces.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.__power_8: The reciprocal of the ballistic coefficient, a property of the entering object.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.beta: The ballistic coefficient of the entering object, which combines mass, drag coefficient, and reference area.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.h: The altitude or height of the object above a reference surface.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.__deriv_3: The rate of change of the object's velocity with respect to its altitude.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.__sin_6: The sine of the flight path angle, indicating the steepness of the trajectory.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.gamma: The flight path angle, representing the angle between the velocity vector and the local horizontal.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.__power_3: The reciprocal of the object's velocity.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.dv: An infinitesimal change in the object's velocity.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.dh: An infinitesimal change in the object's altitude.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.__integral_2: The indefinite integral of the reciprocal of velocity with respect to velocity, which evaluates to $\ln(v)$.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.__integral_4: The indefinite integral of a term related to atmospheric density, ballistic coefficient, and flight path angle with respect to height.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.__log_2: The natural logarithm of the object's velocity.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.__const_3: Euler's number, the base of the natural logarithm.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.C_1: An arbitrary constant of integration arising from the indefinite integral.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.rho_0: The atmospheric density at a reference altitude, typically at sea level.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity._r2___power_8: An exponential term describing the decay of atmospheric density with increasing altitude.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.e: Euler's number, the base of the natural logarithm.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.__power_11: The reciprocal of the atmospheric scale height.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.H: The atmospheric scale height, characterizing how rapidly atmospheric density decreases with altitude.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.__power_13: The reciprocal of the ballistic coefficient, a property of the entering object.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.__power_2: The complete expression for the velocity of the object as a function of altitude, derived from the integration.
Allen–Eggers entry velocity.v_E: The entry velocity, specifically referring to the velocity at which the object enters the atmosphere.

Integrate by separation.__power_3: This term represents the reciprocal of the variable $v$ that is being integrated.
Integrate by separation.v: This is one of the dependent variables being separated for integration.
Integrate by separation.dv: This is the differential of the variable $v$, indicating integration with respect to $v$.
Integrate by separation.dh: This is the differential of the variable $h$, indicating integration with respect to $h$.
Integrate by separation.k: This is a constant coefficient in the expression being integrated with respect to $h$.
Integrate by separation.__power_6: This is an exponential term involving the variable $h$ and the constant $H$.
Integrate by separation.e: This represents Euler's number, the base of the natural logarithm.
Integrate by separation.h: This is the other dependent variable being separated for integration.
Integrate by separation.__power_9: This term represents the reciprocal of the constant $H$ within the exponent.
Integrate by separation.H: This is a constant parameter found in the exponent of the exponential term.
Integrate by separation.__integral_2: This represents the integral of $\frac{1}{v}$ with respect to $v$.
Integrate by separation.__integral_4: This represents the integral of the expression $\left(- e^{\frac{\left(-1\right) h}{H}} k\right)$ with respect to $h$
